---
title: "Probability Class"
subtitle: "Foundations for Public Health & Epidemiological Data Analysis"

author:
  - name: "Ryan Musasia"
    affiliations:
      - "Associate Research Fellow"
      - "Center for Epidemiological Modelling and Analysis"

format:
  live-revealjs:
    theme: cema.scss
    logo: assets/cema_logo.png
    include-in-header: header.html
    slide-number: c/t
    progress: true
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    highlight-style: github
    code-copy: true
    code-line-numbers: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.09

# ---- Orange Africa hero on the title slide (Reveal native bg layer) ----
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: "assets/africa_orange.png"
  data-background-size: "40%"
  data-background-position: "left center"

# ---- WebR (runs entirely in the browser; base R only, for fast start-up) ----
engine: knitr
resources:
  - assets
---

{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

::: {.notes}
Today I will take you through Probability where majority of the concepts in this course stands on — risk, diagnostic testing, and statistical inference are all probability disguise.

Set the tone: This slide deck is interactive. The grey boxes are live R — I'll run them as we go, and because it's hosted online you can open the same link on your laptop, edit the code, and re-run it yourself.
:::

## From data to decisions {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
Probability is the language for uncertainty, and it lets us go from what we measured in a sample to a claim about the whole population."

- Prevalence & risk: a probability is just a proportion of people. "The chance a
  random person is a case" and "the prevalence" are similar.

- Diagnostic testing — explain each in plain words (think of a smoke alarm):
    • Sensitivity — how good the test is at CATCHING people who really are sick.
      Out of 100 people who truly have the disease, how many does it correctly
      call positive?
    • Specificity — how good the test is at giving the ALL-CLEAR to people who are
      NOT sick. Out of 100 healthy people, how many does it correctly call
      negative? (think of an email spam filter, where "positive" = flagged as spam)
      How well it LEAVES YOUR REAL EMAILS ALONE. Of all your genuine
      messages, how many does it correctly keep out of the spam folder? A specific
      filter rarely bins a real email by mistake
    • Positive predictive value (PPV) — it answer's the question: 
    "Of all the people who test positive, what fraction are truly sick."
    • Negative predictive value (NPV) — the mirror: "Of all the people who test negative, 
    what fraction are truly healthy (disease-free)."

- Inference: p-values and confidence intervals are probability statements. If the
  probability model is shaky, the inference is too.
:::

You have already summarise data and fit models. **Probability** is the connective
tissue underneath: it is how we quantify *uncertainty* and move from a sample to
a statement about a population.

In public health it is behind:

- **Prevalence and risk** — a probability is a population proportion.
- **Diagnostic testing** — sensitivity, specificity, and predictive value are all conditional probabilities.
- **Inference** — P-values, confidence intervals, and every model you will fit rest on a probability model for the data.

::: {.callout}
**Every** code block is **live**. Edit it and press *Run* to execute R in your browser.
:::

## Defining probability {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
Two ideas live on this slide

Idea 1 — long-run frequency: The probability of something is the fraction of
times it happens if you repeat the experiment over and over. We can't roll
forever, but that fraction is the target we're heading toward.

Idea 2 — the counting shortcut: "When the outcomes are all equally likely, we
don't need to repeat anything — we just count: favourable outcomes over total
outcomes." The die is perfect because all six faces are equally likely.

Walk the four bullets as an escalating story — each one teaches a new rule:
- P(1) = 1/6 — the base case. Ask the room "what's the chance of a 4?" so they say
  1/6 too and feel the symmetry.
- P(1 or 2) = 2/6 = 1/3 — widen the event to two faces. This is the ADDITION idea;
  note in passing "a die can't show 1 and 2 at once" — quietly introduces mutually
  exclusive events (slide 1.7).
- P(any of 1–6) = 1 — something MUST happen, so probability 1 = certainty. Anchor
  the top of the scale; contrast with rolling a 7 (probability 0) for the bottom.
- P(not a 2) = 5/6 — the COMPLEMENT. Show it two ways: count the five non-2 faces,
  OR do 1 − 1/6. Land the payoff:

Transition: "Let's watch that first idea, long-run frequency actually happen."
:::

Intuitively, the **probability** of an outcome is the proportion of times it
would occur if the experiment were repeated indefinitely. Its a  *long-run
relative frequency*.

When a finite set of outcomes is **equally likely**,

$$P(A)=\frac{\text{number of outcomes in } A}{\text{total number of outcomes}}.$$

A fair die has **six equally likely** faces:

- $$P(\text{roll a } 1) = 1/6$$ — one favourable outcome out of six.
- $$P(1 \text{ or } 2) = 2/6 = 1/3$$ — two favourable outcomes.
- $$P(1,2,3,4,5, \text{ or } 6) = 6/6 = 1$$ — a **certain** event.
- $$P(\text{not a } 2) = 1 - \tfrac{1}{6} = 5/6$$ — **the complement.**

## Defining probability, live {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
Step 1 — run the cell. The four printed lines just confirm the dice numbers from
the last slide (1/6, 1/3, 1, 5/6). Read them off quickly; the plot is the star.

Step 2 — TALK THROUGH THE PLOT:
- Orange is what actually HAPPENED — the running proportion of 1s as rolls pile up.
  Blue dashed is the TRUTH we're chasing: exactly 1/6 ≈ 0.167. Blue isn't a
  simulation; abline() just paints a flat marker at 1/6." Stress: these are
  observed-vs-truth, NOT two competing scenarios (students often assume one line is
  one method, the other another — it isn't).

— HOW THE ROLLING WORKS
1) sample(die, 6000, replace = TRUE) = "draw a face 6,000 times, putting the die
   back each time" — every roll independent, all six faces always available.
2) rolls == 1 marks each roll yes/no (is it a 1?).
3) cumsum(...)/seq_along(...) = running tally of 1s ÷ rolls so far = the orange
   line. (set.seed(1) just makes the rolls reproducible, not less random.)
The very first roll here is a 1, so the line starts at a wild 1.0, then drifts
down as the early luck gets diluted — ending ~0.163, a hair under 1/6.

— WHY JUMPY EARLY, SMOOTH LATE — use the GRADE-AVERAGE analogy (this is the one):
"The orange line behaves exactly like your exam average. Your FIRST exam IS your
average — one mark, 100% of the weight, so it can sit anywhere. A second exam can
still swing it hugely. But after 30 exams, one more paper barely nudges your
average — it's diluted by all the others. Each die roll is just 'one more exam'
added in. 

Few results → volatile average; many results → rock-steady average."

— BANK THE LESSON (say it explicitly): "The gap between orange and blue is
sampling error, and it shrinks as the sample grows. Same reason a prevalence from
20 patients bounces around but one from 5,000 is trustworthy — the seed of
statistical inference."

- Name it: "This is the Law of Large Numbers, and it IS the frequentist
  definition of probability — the long-run proportion converging to the true
  value."

Step 3 — make it interactive. Ask a student: "Change 6000 to 100 and re-run."
It never really settles — the point of small samples. Then change set.seed(1) to
another number (or delete it) and re-run to show a DIFFERENT random path that
still lands near 1/6. Different journeys, same destination.
:::

The **frequentist** definition in action: as we keep rolling, the observed proportion of 1s settles at $1/6$.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
die <- 1:6

# The four introductory examples (theoretical probabilities)
cat("P(roll a 1) =", round(1/6, 4), "\n") # 0.1667
cat("P(1 or 2) =", round(2/6, 4), "\n") # 0.3333
cat("P(any of 1-6) =", 6/6, "\n") # 1  (a certain event)
cat("P(not a 2) =", round(5/6, 4), "\n") # 0.8333

# Frequentist view: simulate rolls and watch P(1) converge to 1/6
set.seed(1)
rolls <- sample(die, 6000, replace = TRUE)
running <- cumsum(rolls == 1) / seq_along(rolls)

plot(running, type = "l", col = "#F58220", lwd = 2, ylim = c(0, 0.4),
     xlab = "Number of rolls", ylab = "Proportion of 1s",
     main = "Long-run frequency approaches 1/6")
abline(h = 1/6, lty = 2, col = "#2DA7E0", lwd = 2)
```

## Our working example {.smaller}

Throughout, we use a **simulated cohort** of 500 people with a smoking exposure
and a disease outcome. Run this, it builds `cohort` data for the examples that
follow. `iris` (built into R) serves as a practice.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
set.seed(2024)
n <- 500
smoke <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.40) # 40% are smokers
dis <- rbinom(n, 1, ifelse(smoke == 1, 0.30, 0.10)) # smokers: higher risk
cohort <- data.frame(
  age = round(rnorm(n, 45, 12)),
  smoker = factor(ifelse(smoke == 1, "yes", "no"),  levels = c("no", "yes")),
  outcome = factor(ifelse(dis == 1, "case", "control"), levels = c("control", "case"))
)
head(cohort, 5)
```

# 1.5 Probability terminology {.section-slide background-image="assets/africa_orange.png" background-size="42%" background-position="left"}

## Sample space and events

::: {.notes}
"Before we can talk about the probability of something, we have to be
clear about what CAN happen. That full list of possibilities is the sample space."

Define the two terms concretely:
- Sample space (S) = the set of ALL possible outcomes of an experiment. Event =
  any subset of S — one outcome, several, or none.
- If the outcomes are all equally likely, each single one has probability 1/|S|
  (|S| = how many outcomes there are). Tie back to the die: S = {1,2,3,4,5,6},
  |S| = 6, each face 1/6.

The subtlety worth flagging (from the iris warm-up): the sample space depends on
WHAT YOU DECIDE TO RECORD. "Pick a flower and note its SPECIES" → S = {setosa,
versicolor, virginica}, just three outcomes, so P(setosa) = 1/3. 

But "pick a flower and note ALL its measurements" → a huge sample space. Same flower,
different experiment, different S. Good 20-second point: you define the experiment,
you define the sample space.

Simple vs compound:
- Simple event = a single outcome. e.g. "the person I drew is a case."
- Compound event = more than one outcome bundled together. e.g. "case AND smoker,"
  or "Sepal.Length > 5" (which is true for MANY flowers, so it's compound).

Bridge to epidemiology (say it): "Our experiment this session is 'draw a random
person from the cohort.' The sample space is every person; an event is any group
we care about — the cases, the smokers, the over-50s. Probability is just how big
that group is relative to everyone."

Question to the room: "If I draw one person and record only their disease status,
what's my sample space?" (Answer: {case, control} — two outcomes.) "Now if I also
record smoking status?" (Four combinations.) Shows how recording more expands S.


:::


The **sample space** $S$ is the set of all possible outcomes of an experiment.
An **event** is any subset of $S$. If all $|S|$ outcomes are equally likely, each
has probability $1/|S|$.

- A **simple event** is a single outcome (e.g. a randomly chosen person is a *case*).
- A **compound event** combines several outcomes (e.g. *case* **and** *smoker*).

::: {.callout}
We'll use the `iris` dataset:


The experiment **"Pick a random flower and note its species"**
has sample space {setosa, versicolor, virginica}.
:::

- If species are equally likely,

- $$P(\text{setosa}) = 1/3$$

## Events as probabilities in R {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
Purpose (~2–3 min): the single most useful coding idea of the day.
Lead: "In R, an event is just a TRUE/FALSE question about each person, and mean()
of that gives the proportion that are TRUE — which IS the probability."
- Run it. iris warm-up: mean(Sepal.Length > 5) = 0.787. Then epi: P(case) = 0.172
  (the prevalence), P(smoker) = 0.436.
- Hammer the fragment: probability and proportion are the SAME object. "0.172 is
  both 'the probability a random person is a case' AND 'the prevalence.'"
Question: "How would you write P(age > 60)?" (mean(cohort$age > 60).)
Transition: "Now let's combine events — AND, OR, NOT." → next.
:::

An event is a logical condition. Its probability is the **mean of that logical
vector** (the proportion of `TRUE`s).

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
# Practice: P(Sepal.Length > 5) — a simple event on iris dataset
mean(iris$Sepal.Length > 5)

# Epidemiology Example: P(case) is the disease PREVALENCE in the cohort
mean(cohort$outcome == "case") # a probability IS a proportion

# P(smoker) — the exposure prevalence
mean(cohort$smoker == "yes")
```

::: {.fragment}
`mean(outcome == "case")` returns **0.172** is the prevalence. In epidemiology a probability and a population proportion are similar.
:::

# Set operations on events {.section-slide background-image="assets/africa_orange.png" background-size="42%" background-position="left"}

## Combining events {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
- Walk the table: union (OR), intersection (AND), difference (but not), complement
  (not). Sketch a two-circle Venn with your hands.
- The union MINUS term is the one to explain: "why subtract the overlap? People who
  are BOTH get counted in P(A) and again in P(B) — subtract them once to fix the
  double-count."
- Complement = the workhorse shortcut (callback to dice 'not a 2': 1 − P).
- Empty set = impossible event, probability 0.
Question: "In a Venn diagram, which region is A − B?" (A, minus the overlap.)
Transition: "Let's compute all four on the cohort, one line each." → next.
:::


| Operation | Notation | Meaning | Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| Union | $A \cup B$ | $A$ **or** $B$ (or both) | $P(A\cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A\cap B)$ |
| Intersection | $A \cap B$ | $A$ **and** $B$ | $P(A\cap B)=P(A)\,P(B\mid A)$ |
| Difference | $A - B$ | $A$ but not $B$ | $P(A-B)=P(A)-P(A\cap B)$ |
| Complement | $A'$ | not $A$ | $P(A')=1-P(A)$ |

The union rule subtracts $P(A\cap B)$ so the overlap is not counted twice. The
empty event $\varnothing$ has $P(\varnothing)=0$.

## Set operations in R {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
- Run the cell. Point out the operators: | = OR (union), & = AND (intersection),
  ! = NOT (complement), & ! = difference.
- Read results: P(smoker)=0.436, P(case)=0.172, union 0.482, intersect 0.126,
  smoker-only 0.310, not-case 0.828.
- Do the union-rule check LIVE (the fragment): 0.436 + 0.172 − 0.126 = 0.482 —
  exactly mean(S | D). "Formula and direct count agree — the addition rule working."
Question: get a student to predict P(smoker OR case) before you reveal it, then check.
Transition: "We've been doing favourable-over-total without naming it — let's make
the definition explicit." → next.
:::

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
S <- cohort$smoker  == "yes" # event: smoker
D <- cohort$outcome == "case" # event: diseased (case)

c(P_smoker = mean(S),
  P_case = mean(D),
  P_union = mean(S | D), # smoker OR case
  P_intersect = mean(S & D), # smoker AND case
  P_smoker_only = mean(S & !D), # smoker but not a case
  P_not_case = mean(!D)) # complement of case
```

::: {.fragment}
Check the union rule: $$P(S)+P(D)-P(S\cap D) = 0.436 + 0.172 - 0.126 = 0.482$$ **Equalling** `mean(S | D)`.
:::

# 1.6 Definition of probability {.section-slide background-image="assets/africa_orange.png" background-size="42%" background-position="left"}

## What a probability is {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
- Classical formula: favourable ÷ total (already intuitive from the die).
- Three axioms in plain words: probabilities are never negative; something in S is
  certain (P(S)=1); for disjoint events, probabilities add. "Everything in
  probability is built from these three."
- Frequentist reading → why mean() estimates a probability, and why bigger samples
  are steadier (callback to the die plot).
- Run the cell: prevalence estimated from n = 20, 100, 500 — watch it steady as n
  grows. Re-run to show the n=20 estimate jumping around.
Question: "Which estimate would you trust — n=20 or n=500? Why?"
Transition: "One more term before conditioning — events that can't co-occur." → next.
:::


Classically, for equally likely outcomes,
$$P(A) = \frac{\text{number of outcomes in } A}{\text{total number of outcomes in } S}.$$

Every probability obeys three axioms: 

- $0 \le P(A) \le 1$; $P(S)=1$; and 
- for disjoint events probabilities add. 
- The **frequentist** reading is, a long-run proportion over many repetitions, which is why `mean(condition)` estimates a
probability, and **why larger samples give more stable estimates.**

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
# Estimate P(case) from samples of increasing size
for (m in c(20, 100, 500)) {
  s <- sample(cohort$outcome, m, replace = TRUE)
  cat("n =", m, "  estimated prevalence =", round(mean(s == "case"), 3), "\n")
}
```

# 1.7 Mutually exclusive events {.section-slide background-image="assets/africa_orange.png" background-size="42%" background-position="left"}

## Events that cannot co-occur {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
- "Mutually exclusive = can't both happen. Then P(A∩B)=0 and OR simply ADDS."
- Case vs control: mutually exclusive (nobody is both) → run it: mean(case & control)=0.
- BUT smoker & case are NOT exclusive → 0.126. "And that's the whole point: because
  they CAN co-occur, they can be ASSOCIATED. If exposure and disease were mutually
  exclusive, smoking would perfectly PREVENT disease."
Question: "Name two health events that ARE mutually exclusive, and two that AREN'T."
Transition: "When events can co-occur, we ask — given one, what's the chance of the
other? That's conditioning." → next.
:::

Two events are **mutually exclusive** (disjoint) when they cannot happen
together: $A \cap B = \varnothing$, so $P(A\cap B)=0$ and the addition rule
simplifies to
$$P(A\cup B)=P(A)+P(B).$$

A person's disease status is *case* or *control*, never both. Those categories are mutually exclusive. Exposure and disease, however, are **not** disjoint (a person can be both a smoker and a case), which is exactly why they can be *associated*.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
# Mutually exclusive: no one is both a case AND a control
mean(cohort$outcome == "case" & cohort$outcome == "control")   

# NOT mutually exclusive: smoker and case co-occur
mean(cohort$smoker == "yes" & cohort$outcome == "case") 
```

# 1.8 Conditional probability {.section-slide background-image="assets/africa_orange.png" background-size="42%" background-position="left"}

## Conditioning on information {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
- "Conditioning = shrink the world to just the group where B is true, then ask
  about A inside it." P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B).
- Concrete: "P(case | smoker) — ignore everyone else, look only at smokers; what
  fraction are cases?"
- Rearrange → multiplication rule P(A∩B)=P(B)·P(A|B). Tie to the probability tree:
  "multiply along the branches — THIS is that rule."
- Epi framing: risk in the exposed, survival given treatment, disease given a
  positive test — all conditional probabilities.
Question: "Is P(smoker | case) the same as P(case | smoker)?" (NO — order matters.
Plants the seed for Bayes.)
Transition: "Let's see marginal, joint, and conditional all in one table." → next.
:::

The probability of $A$ **given** that $B$ occurred restricts attention to the
world where $B$ is true:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \qquad P(B) > 0.$$

Rearranging gives the **general multiplication rule**
$P(A\cap B)=P(B)\,P(A\mid B)$. Conditioning is necessary in epidemiology:

- **risk** *in the exposed*,
- **survival** *given* treatment,
- **disease** *given* a positive test.

## Contingency tables: marginal, joint, conditional {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
- Run the cell. Three outputs: counts; prop.table (JOINT — all cells sum to 1);
  prop.table(margin=1) (CONDITIONAL — each row sums to 1).
- Define on the table: marginal = the totals (overall prevalence); joint = a single
  cell (P(smoker AND case)); conditional = a cell relative to its row
  (P(case | smoker)).
- The fragment: the bottom row of the last output IS P(case | smoker) = 0.289 —
  point straight at it.
- Callback: the table and the probability tree hold the same information two ways.
Question: "Which output gives the overall prevalence?" (The margins — marginal.)
Transition: "Now the payoff — compare that conditional across groups and you get
RISK." → next.
:::

A 2×2 table holds all three probability types at once:

- **marginal** (row/column totals),
- **joint** (a single cell), and
- **conditional** (a cell relative to its row or column).

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
tab <- table(cohort$smoker, cohort$outcome)
tab
prop.table(tab) # JOINT probabilities (cells sum to 1)
prop.table(tab, margin = 1) # CONDITIONAL on smoker: P(outcome | smoker)
```

::: {.fragment}
The bottom row of the last output is $P(\text{case}\mid\text{smoker})$ 
:::

## Conditional probability *is* risk {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
- Run the cell. Risk in smokers 0.289, in non-smokers 0.082, ratio 3.54.
- "This IS an epidemiological analysis in three lines: risk in the exposed ÷ risk
  in the unexposed. Smokers are 3.5× as likely to be cases."
- Stress: it's a ratio of two conditional probabilities — everything we built leads
  here.
- Honest caveat: association ≠ causation. Here the association is real by design
  (simulated); in real data you'd worry about confounding.
Question: "If the risk ratio were 1.0, what would that mean?" (No association →
independence, next slide.)
Transition: "A ratio of 1 means the two are independent — let's define that and its
famous trap." → next.
:::

Comparing a conditional probability across exposure groups gives the **risk ratio** — the measure of association in epidemiology.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
p_exposed   <- mean(cohort$outcome[cohort$smoker == "yes"] == "case")
p_unexposed <- mean(cohort$outcome[cohort$smoker == "no"]  == "case")

cat("Risk | smoker =", round(p_exposed,   3), "\n")
cat("Risk | non-smoker =", round(p_unexposed, 3), "\n")
cat("Risk ratio =", round(p_exposed / p_unexposed, 2), "\n")
```

::: {.fragment}
Smokers are **3.5 times** as likely to be cases. That single number, a ratio of two conditional probabilities is a fundamental measure in epidemiological analysis.
:::

## Independence and the gambler's fallacy {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
- Independent = conditioning changes nothing: P(A|B)=P(A), equivalently
  P(A∩B)=P(A)·P(B).
- "If smoking and disease were independent, knowing someone smokes wouldn't change
  their risk — risk ratio 1."
- Gambler's fallacy: independent trials have NO memory. Five reds in a row doesn't
  make black 'due.' Each roll, flip, or patient is a fresh draw (callback: no die
  roll remembers the last).
- Clinical relevance: a second INDEPENDENT test doesn't 'balance out' the first.
Question: "A coin lands heads 4 times — P(heads) next toss?" (Still 1/2.)
Transition: "The most important conditional-probability skill in medicine is
flipping one around — enter Bayes." → next.
:::

$A$ and $B$ are **independent** when conditioning changes nothing:
$$P(A\mid B)=P(A) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad P(A\cap B)=P(A)\,P(B).$$

If exposure and disease were independent, the risk ratio would be 1 — no association. Independence also has a famous trap, past independent outcomes carry **no** information about the next one (***The gambler's fallacy***). 

Each new patient, coin flip, or test is a fresh draw.

# Bayes' theorem & diagnostic testing {.section-slide background-image="assets/africa_orange.png" background-size="42%" background-position="left"}

::: {.notes}
Question for the class: Before we continue, what do you think is the main difference between a Frequentist and a Bayesian way of thinking about probability?

Expected answer (simple):


Frequentist: Probability is the long-run frequency of an event if we repeated the same experiment many times. The unknown parameter is fixed, but the data vary.


Bayesian: Probability represents our degree of belief about an event or parameter. We begin with what we already know (a prior) and update that belief with new evidence to obtain a posterior probability.


Simple example:
Imagine a doctor suspects a patient has a disease.
A Frequentist asks: "If we repeated this test on thousands of similar patients, how often would it be correct?"
A Bayesian asks: "Given what we already know about how common the disease is and this patient's positive test result, what is the probability that this patient actually has the disease?"
Key takeaway: Frequentists learn from repeated experiments, while Bayesians learn by updating beliefs as new evidence becomes available.
Transition: "This Bayesian way of updating our beliefs is exactly what we'll use next to understand why a highly accurate test can still produce many false positives."
:::


## Inverting a conditional probability {.smaller}
::: {.notes}
- The problem: a test gives P(positive | disease), but the patient needs
  P(disease | positive) — the REVERSE. These are not the same (callback: order
  matters, slide 17).
- Bayes flips it. Show the formula but don't dwell on algebra — the denominator is
  just "all the ways to test positive."
- Re-anchor the four terms plainly (spam-filter/smoke-alarm): sensitivity,
  specificity, prevalence, and the target we want — PPV.
Question: "Why isn't P(disease | positive) just the test's sensitivity?" (Because
false positives from the huge healthy majority flood in — segue.)
Transition: "Let's make that concrete with a headcount before we compute." → next.
:::

A test tells us $P(\text{positive}\mid\text{disease})$, but a clinician needs the
**inverse**, $P(\text{disease}\mid\text{positive})$.

**Bayes' theorem** flips it:

$$P(A\mid B)=\frac{P(B\mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B\mid A)P(A)+P(B\mid A')P(A')}.$$

In screening language: 

- **sensitivity** $=P(+\mid \text{disease})$,
- **specificity** $=P(-\mid \text{no disease})$,
- **prevalence** $=P(\text{disease})$,

and the answer we want is the **positive predictive value** $P(\text{disease}\mid +)$.

## The diagnostic 2×2 table {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
- Walk the headcount: 100,000 people, 0.35% prevalence → 350 sick, 99,650 healthy.
  Test catches 312 true positives, but misfires on 6,975 healthy people (false
  positives); 7,287 test positive in total.
- If using the colour 2×2 image: DOWN the columns = sensitivity/specificity
  (properties of the test); ACROSS the rows = PPV/NPV (what your result means).
  Same 312 true positives feed both sensitivity (312/350 = 89%) and PPV
  (312/7,287 = 4.3%) — only the denominator changed.
- Land it: "Of 7,287 positives, only 312 are truly sick — the false positives from
  the huge healthy crowd swamp them."
Question (before revealing): "Guess — of everyone who tests positive, what % are
actually sick?" (Most guess high; reveal 4.3% next slide.)
Transition: "Let's confirm that 4.3% straight from the probabilities." → next.
:::

Picture screening **100,000 people** with this test — 89%
sensitivity, 93% specificity, for a disease with **0.35% prevalence**. Everyone
lands in one of four cells:

|                   | Disease **present** | Disease **absent** | Total |
|-------------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Test positive** | 312 (TP) | 6,975 (FP) | **7,287** |
| **Test negative** | 38 (FN) | 92,675 (TN) | 92,713 |
| **Total**         | 350 | 99,650 | 100,000 |

- **Sensitivity** $=\frac{TP}{TP+FN}=\frac{312}{350}\approx 0.89$ — of the diseased, who tests positive.
- **Specificity** $=\frac{TN}{TN+FP}=\frac{92{,}675}{99{,}650}\approx 0.93$ — of the healthy, who tests negative.
- **PPV** $=\frac{TP}{TP+FP}=\frac{312}{7{,}287}\approx 0.043$ — of the test-positives, who is truly sick.

Of the **7,287** people who test positive, only **312** are actually ill, the
other **6,975 are false positives**.


## Why a positive test can still mean low risk {.smaller}

A good test applied to a **rare** disease produces mostly false positives. Run this, then lower `prevalence` and watch the predictive value.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
sensitivity <- 0.89 # P(test + | disease)
specificity <- 0.93 # P(test - | no disease)
prevalence  <- 0.0035 # P(disease) -- try 0.01, 0.05, 0.20

ppv <- sensitivity * prevalence /
       (sensitivity * prevalence + (1 - specificity) * (1 - prevalence))

cat("Positive predictive value =", round(ppv, 4),
    " (", round(100 * ppv, 1), "% )\n", sep = "")
```

::: {.fragment}
At 0.35% prevalence the PPV is just **4.3%** — 24 of every 25 positives are false
alarms. This is why rare conditions are confirmed with a second, independent test.
:::

## A historical epidemic: Smallpox, Boston 1721 {.smaller}

Real data on 6,224 residents during a smallpox outbreak — did **inoculation**
(a precursor to vaccination) reduce the risk of death?

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
smallpox <- matrix(c(238, 5136,
                       6,  844),
                   nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                   dimnames = list(result = c("lived", "died"),
                                   inoculated = c("yes", "no")))
smallpox
cat("P(died | inoculated) =", round(6   / 244,  4), "\n")  # 0.0246
cat("P(died | not inoculated) =", round(844 / 5980, 4), "\n")  # 0.1411
```

::: {.fragment}
Death was about **1 in 40** for the inoculated versus **1 in 7** not innoculated. 

- An early quantitative case for ***immunisation***.
:::

# Random variables {.section-slide background-image="assets/africa_orange.png" background-size="42%" background-position="left"}

## From events to numbers {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
- "A random variable just attaches a NUMBER to each outcome — days in hospital,
  new cases this week, cost of care."
- Expected value = probability-weighted average = the long-run mean.
  E(X) = Σ x·P(x).
- Run the cell: length of stay, E(X) = 0.7 days. "Average this over thousands of
  patients and you'd get 0.7."
- Epi meaning: expectation = expected BURDEN — bed-days per patient, cases per week,
  cost per episode — how you scale to a population.
Question: "No single patient stays 0.7 days — so what does 0.7 mean?" (An average
over many, not any one person.)
Transition: "The average isn't the whole story — two wards with the same mean can
feel very different. That's variance." → next.
:::

A **random variable** $X$ attaches a number to each outcome, example days of hospital stay, number of new cases, cost of care.

Its **expected value** is the probability-weighted average, the long-run mean:
$$E(X)=\sum_i x_i\,P(X=x_i).$$

Expectation is how we turn a probability distribution into an **expected burden**, also cases per week, bed-days per patient, cost per episode.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
x <- c(0, 1, 2, 3) # days of stay
p <- c(0.55, 0.25, 0.15, 0.05)  # probabilities (sum to 1)

Ex <- sum(x * p)
cat("E(X) = expected stay =", Ex, "days\n")
```

## Variability: variance and standard deviation {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
- Variance = probability-weighted average of SQUARED distances from the mean;
  SD = its square root (back in the original units — 'days').
- Run the cell: E(X)=0.7, Var=0.81, SD=0.9 days; the barplot shows the distribution.
- Key point: "Two interventions can share the SAME expected outcome but different
  spread — and spread IS risk. Same average, bigger variance = less predictable."
- Use SD, not variance, to talk to people — it's in the same units as the data.
Question: "Two wards both average 0.7 days; one has SD 0.2, the other SD 2 — which
would you rather manage?"
Transition: "That's the toolkit — let's pull it together." → next.
:::

The **variance** measures how much outcomes scatter around $E(X)$, weighting
squared deviations by their probability:
$$\operatorname{Var}(X)=\sum_i (x_i-\mu)^2\,P(X=x_i), \qquad \sigma=\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(X)}.$$

Two interventions can share the same expected outcome yet differ sharply in
**risk** — the spread. Variance is how we quantify that uncertainty.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
x <- c(0, 1, 2, 3); p <- c(0.55, 0.25, 0.15, 0.05)
mu <- sum(x * p)
v <- sum((x - mu)^2 * p)
cat("E(X)  =", mu, " days\n")
cat("Var(X)=", v,  "\n")
cat("SD(X) =", sqrt(v), " days\n")

bp <- barplot(p, names.arg = x, col = "#2DA7E0", border = "white",
              xlab = "Days of stay", ylab = "Probability",
              main = sprintf("Length of stay (mean = %.1f days)", mu))
```

# Wrap-up {.section-slide background-image="assets/africa_orange.png" background-size="42%" background-position="left"}

## Recap {.smaller}

::: {.notes}
- A probability is a proportion — in R, mean(condition). Prevalence and risk are
  probabilities.
- Set operations + the addition rule combine events; mutually exclusive events add.
- Conditional probability is the engine of epi; comparing it across groups gives the
  risk ratio.
- Bayes turns test accuracy into predictive value — and warns about false positives
  when disease is rare.
- Random variables summarise numeric outcomes via expectation (burden) and variance
  (uncertainty).
The single thread: "every one of these was a proportion or a weighted average —
probability is just careful counting." Preview: distributions (binomial, Poisson,
normal) and statistical inference next.
Question: "Which of these will you use most in your own work?" (Gets them to
self-connect.)
Transition: → close.
:::


- A **probability is a proportion** — in R, `mean(condition)`. Prevalence and risk are probabilities.
- **Set operations** (∪, ∩, −, complement) and the addition rule combine events; **mutually exclusive** events simply add.
- **Conditional probability** $P(A\mid B)$ is the engine of epidemiology; comparing it across groups gives the **risk ratio**.
- **Bayes' theorem** turns test accuracy (sensitivity, specificity) into **predictive value** — and warns us about false positives when disease is rare.
- **Random variables** summarise numeric outcomes through **expectation** (expected burden) and **variance** (uncertainty).

::: {.callout}
Next: probability distributions (binomial, Poisson, normal) and the move into
statistical inference.
:::

# Thank you {.section-slide background-image="assets/africa_orange.png" background-size="42%" background-position="left"}
